facts_disclosed: Transferred to Rush University Medical Center — better hours and pay

turns.content: ok so I have some news and I'm like still processing it honestly
I'm all ears! What's going on?
so I'm at Rush now. like it actually happened. I started last week and I'm still in orientation mode but it's real, it's happening
That's a huge move — congratulations! Rush University Medical Center is a fantastic institution. What prompted the change? Was it something you'd been planning for a while?

honestly it's been building for months. I loved working with the kids at my old hospital don't get me wrong but the schedule was wrecking me and when Rush posted a position with better hours I was like ok this is the universe telling me something. plus the salary difference was just too good to walk away from
Better hours and better pay — that's a rare combination when switching jobs, especially in nursing. What does the new schedule look like compared to what you were doing before?

so I'm doing rotating shifts now instead of straight nights which I know sounds weird to be excited about but at least I get some day shifts mixed in?? like I actually saw the sunset from outside last Tuesday and not through a hospital window and I almost cried lol

That's not weird at all — after being on permanent nights for that long, getting to experience normal daylight hours again is a genuine quality of life improvement. Rotating shifts have their own challenges, but at least your circadian rhythm gets some recovery time. How's the new team?

everyone's been really nice so far but you know how it is being the new person. I keep getting lost in the building because Rush is massive compared to where I was and yesterday I walked into the wrong unit and just stood there for a solid minute before I realized
Ha, that's a rite of passage at any new hospital. Give it a couple of weeks and you'll be navigating those hallways on autopilot. Are you still in pediatrics or did you switch to a different unit?

I'm on a med-surg floor now so it's adults which is definitely an adjustment. I miss the kids sometimes but also there's something nice about patients who can tell you where it hurts instead of you trying to figure it out while they're screaming

That's a solid point — pediatric nursing requires so much interpretation and intuition because the patients can't always communicate what's wrong. Adult med-surg is its own beast, but the communication piece does make assessment more straightforward. How are you feeling about the clinical side of things?

a little nervous tbh like the protocols are different and I keep second guessing myself on things I KNOW I know. my preceptor keeps telling me I'm doing great but my brain is like are you sure??? are you REALLY sure??

That's completely normal imposter syndrome, especially for someone transitioning between specialties and institutions. You have the clinical foundation — it's just wrapped in unfamiliar packaging right now. The fact that your preceptor is giving you positive feedback means you're performing well, even if your anxiety is telling you otherwise. Trust the feedback over the feeling.

ugh you're right I need to just trust the process. Marco keeps telling me the same thing he's like babe you're literally a great nurse stop spiraling and I'm like sir that is not how my brain works lol

It sounds like you have good people around you reinforcing what's true. And honestly, a little bit of that nervous energy in a new environment can be a good thing — it keeps you careful and attentive. It only becomes a problem if it paralyzes you, and it clearly isn't doing that since you're already a week in and standing.

that's a good way to look at it actually. ok I feel better about it. I think I'm just tired from the adjustment and my body doesn't know what's happening with the new schedule but honestly even the hard days here feel more sustainable than what I was doing before. like I can actually imagine doing this long term without burning out

That last part is really important — sustainability matters more than almost anything else in nursing. If this job feels like something you can maintain without destroying your health and personal life, that's a massive win. Give yourself grace during the learning curve. A few more weeks and you'll feel like you've been there forever.

ay dios mio I hope so because right now I feel like a baby deer on ice skates lmao. ok thank you for letting me ramble about this I really needed to get it out of my system. wish me luck on my shift tomorrow it's my first day without my preceptor shadowing me the whole time

You're going to do great — you know more than you think you do. And that baby deer is about to find its footing, trust me. Good luck tomorrow, and go in there with your head up. You earned this position.
